name: Ceuta and Melilla
history: Ceuta and Melilla are two Spanish autonomous cities located on the North African coast of Morocco, with origins tracing back to medieval times when they served as strategic military fortifications. Ceuta was established as a Spanish possession in 1668 after being ceded by the Portuguese, while Melilla fell under Spanish control in 1497 during the period of Christian reconquest. Both cities developed as important trading posts and military strongholds, reinforcing Spain's geopolitical presence in the Mediterranean and North Africa. Their status as Spanish enclaves has remained a unique feature of the Iberian Peninsula's political geography for centuries.
culturalNotes: These cities represent a fascinating blend of Spanish, Moroccan, and broader Mediterranean influences, with a diverse population that includes Muslims, Christians, and Jews living in close proximity. The local culture reflects centuries of coexistence between different communities, evident in the architecture, cuisine, and religious sites that dot both cities. What makes Ceuta and Melilla truly distinct is their liminal character as Spanish territory in Africa, creating a unique identity that is neither wholly European nor wholly North African, with vibrant street life and markets that showcase this cultural crossroads.
bestTimeToVisit: Spring and autumn offer comfortable temperatures for exploring these North African enclaves.
languageNotes: Spanish is the official language and primary means of communication in both cities, though Moroccan Arabic and Darija are widely spoken by the sizable Moroccan population. Visitors will find that Spanish predominates in official contexts and among the general populace, though knowledge of basic Arabic phrases can be helpful when interacting with local merchants and residents in the medinas.

places.name: Ceuta
places.description: A Spanish autonomous city on the North African coast facing Gibraltar across the strait, blending Spanish, Amazigh, Jewish and Muslim influences. Its compact old town, forts and seaside promenades make it a fascinating stop where Europe and Africa meet.

activities.detail: Wander this vast Renaissance-era fortification system with moats and drawbridges that once defended the city from land attack.

activities.text: Visit the Foso de San Felipe
activities.detail: Walk across the dramatic dry moat carved into rock, one of the most photogenic parts of the old defensive network.
Hike or drive up this hill for panoramic views over the Strait of Gibraltar and the Rock on a clear day.

activities.text: Tour the Museo de la Legión
activities.detail: Learn about the Spanish Legion's history and its deep ties to Ceuta's identity through uniforms, medals and memorabilia.

activities.text: Relax at Parque Marítimo del Mediterráneo
activities.detail: Swim in seawater pools designed by César Manrique, set along a landscaped seafront promenade.
Visit this coastal fort at the tip of Monte Hacho that now houses a small military museum.
Admire the cathedral and the Hercules gate framing this central square where Spanish and North African styles meet.

activities.text: Wander the old Jewish and Muslim quarters
activities.detail: Walk narrow streets where synagogues, mosques and churches sit close together, reflecting Ceuta's multicultural history.

food.detail: Freshly fried small fish, a Andalusian-Ceutí staple best enjoyed at a seafront chiringuito.
A local take on Moroccan couscous, often served with vegetables and lamb, showing the city's North African influence.
A sweet-savory Moroccan-style pastry filled with spiced meat or chicken and dusted with sugar and cinnamon.
Sweet mint tea served alongside almond pastries, a common afternoon ritual in Ceuta's cafés.
Locally caught squid, usually grilled or fried, reflecting the city's strong fishing tradition.
A classic Spanish breakfast treat enjoyed at cafés around Plaza de los Reyes.

culture.detail: As a border city between Spain and Morocco, police checks are more frequent than on the mainland.
Ceuta hosts Christian, Muslim, Jewish and Hindu communities, so dress modestly near places of worship.
Many visitors combine a Ceuta trip with a day trip to nearby Morocco via the land border at Tarajal.

culture.text: Euro is the currency, not dirham
culture.detail: Despite proximity to Morocco, Ceuta uses the euro, so exchange money before crossing borders.

places.name: Royal Walls of Ceuta
places.description: A massive fortification system dating back to Byzantine and later Portuguese and Spanish rule, encircling the historic core of Ceuta. The moats, drawbridges and bastions offer one of the best-preserved military architecture complexes in the western Mediterranean.

activities.text: Walk the Foso de San Felipe
activities.detail: Stroll along the deep water-filled moat that once made Ceuta's land approach virtually impregnable.

activities.text: Cross the Puente de San Felipe
activities.detail: Cross the reconstructed drawbridge that leads into the old fortified city, echoing centuries of sieges.

activities.text: Visit the Centro de Interpretación de las Murallas Reales
activities.detail: Explore exhibits inside the ramparts explaining the Byzantine, Portuguese and Spanish phases of construction.

activities.text: Climb the Baluarte de la Bandera
activities.detail: Ascend this flag bastion for sweeping views over the moat, harbor and the Strait of Gibraltar.
Admire the medieval keep tower that anchors the fortress complex and once served as a last line of defense.
Time your visit for late afternoon when the stone walls glow gold against the sea.

food.detail: Try this Ceuta specialty, a baked chickpea-flour cake seasoned with cumin, unique to the city's Andalusian-Maghrebi blend of cuisine.
Sip sweet Moroccan mint tea paired with almond pastries at a café near the walls, reflecting Ceuta's cross-strait culture.
Sample lightly fried small fish, a coastal favorite served in nearby seafront restaurants.
Finish with these anise-and-orange fried pastries dusted in sugar, a local sweet often linked to festive occasions.
Look for these crispy shrimp fritters, a North African-influenced snack popular in Ceuta's tapas bars.

culture.detail: The Royal Walls and moat area are generally open to walk through at no cost, making them easy to combine with a city stroll.
Some sections remain active government or ceremonial spaces, so follow posted signs and avoid restricted zones.
Ceuta's population mixes Spanish, Amazigh and Muslim traditions, visible in the food and architecture surrounding the fortress.
Since Ceuta is a Spanish enclave bordering Morocco, carry proper ID as border checks can occur close to the old town.

places.name: Mount Hacho
places.description: A rugged headland believed by some to be one of the mythical Pillars of Hercules, crowned by the Fort of Hacho and a hermitage. Sweeping views stretch across the Strait of Gibraltar to Europe on clear days.

activities.detail: This centuries-old fortress crowning the summit is still used by the Spanish military, so visits are often limited to the exterior and surrounding ramparts.

activities.text: Visit the Ermita de San Antonio
activities.detail: A small whitewashed hermitage near the peak offers a quiet stop and is the focus of a lively local pilgrimage each June.

activities.text: Take in the Strait of Gibraltar viewpoint
activities.detail: On clear days you can see across the water to Gibraltar and the Jebel Musa headland in Morocco, the two candidates for the mythical Pillars of Hercules.

activities.text: Drive or hike the Monte Hacho ring road
activities.detail: A winding road circles the headland through pine woods, giving changing views of Ceuta, the port, and the African coast.
This sandy beach sits at the foot of the mountain and is a good spot to cool off after the climb.
Scattered gun emplacements and watchtowers from Spain's colonial-era defenses dot the slopes and make for atmospheric photos.

food.detail: Crisp shrimp fritters typical of Ceuta's Andalusian-Moroccan fusion cuisine, best enjoyed at a seaside chiringuito near the mountain's base.
Lightly battered fried squid rings are a staple appetizer in Ceuta's port-side restaurants overlooking the strait.
Sweet mint tea served with almond pastries reflects the strong Moroccan cultural influence found throughout Ceuta.
Crescent-shaped almond pastries dusted with sugar, a treat sold in local bakeries that blend Spanish and North African baking traditions.
An assortment of small fried fish fresh from the Strait waters, a classic way to sample the local catch.

culture.detail: Parts of the Fort of Hacho remain in military use, so photography and access may be restricted in certain areas.
As a functioning religious site, the Ermita de San Antonio expects visitors to be quiet and appropriately dressed, especially during Mass or the June pilgrimage.

culture.text: Expect a blend of Spanish and Moroccan customs
culture.detail: Ceuta's unique position as a Spanish exclave in North Africa means local life mixes Catholic and Muslim traditions, visible in food, festivals, and daily rhythms.

culture.text: Go early for clear strait views
culture.detail: Haze often builds over the Strait of Gibraltar by midday, so morning visits offer the best chance to see across to Europe.

places.name: Mediterranean Sea Park
places.description: A striking seafront park designed by César Manrique featuring saltwater pools, gardens and sculptural walkways along the coast. It is a relaxing spot to swim and admire views of the Mediterranean and nearby mountains.

activities.detail: Cool off in the seawater-fed pools that Manrique carved into the rocky shoreline, designed to mimic natural lagoons.
Wander the white, undulating pathways and volcanic-stone terraces that echo Manrique's Lanzarote style.

activities.text: Relax in the palm and cactus gardens
activities.detail: Explore lush landscaped areas filled with palms, succulents and Mediterranean flora between the pools and walkways.

activities.text: Watch sunset over the Strait of Gibraltar
activities.detail: Find a spot along the seafront terraces for views across the water toward the mountains of Morocco.

activities.text: Visit the artificial lake and waterfall features
activities.detail: Discover small cascades and reflective pools integrated into the park's artistic design.
Capture the interplay of white architecture, blue water and Ceuta's mountainous backdrop from the park's highest terrace.

food.detail: Fresh sardines grilled simply with olive oil and salt, a classic seaside snack near the park's food kiosks.
Lightly battered fried squid rings, popular at nearby chiringuitos overlooking the Mediterranean.
A savory-sweet pastry with Moroccan influence, filled with chicken or seafood and dusted with cinnamon sugar.
Sip traditional Moroccan-influenced mint tea at an outdoor café near the park, reflecting Ceuta's cross-cultural heritage.
Sample small plates of local catch like boquerones or gambas at terraces just outside the park's entrance.

culture.detail: The saltwater pools have set opening times, often closing in the evening, so plan swims accordingly.

culture.text: Dress modestly beyond the pool area
culture.detail: While swimwear is fine poolside, cover up when walking through the surrounding public gardens and streets.
Notice how the park blends art and nature, a hallmark of César Manrique's work seen elsewhere in the Canary Islands.

culture.text: Enjoy the mix of Spanish and Moroccan influences
culture.detail: Ceuta's unique cultural blend is reflected in the park's visitors, cuisine, and nearby architecture.

places.name: Moat of San Felipe
places.description: An impressive defensive moat carved into rock, part of the Royal Walls complex separating the peninsula from the mainland. It showcases centuries of fortification engineering and is a highlight for history enthusiasts.

activities.detail: Descend into the deep rock-cut moat to appreciate the scale of the 16th-century fortification engineering up close.
Explore the adjoining Museo de las Murallas Reales housed within the fortress complex to learn about Ceuta's military history.
Capture the imposing stone bastions and restored drawbridge that once controlled access to the old city.
Continue on foot to the nearby Plaza de África, the historic heart of Ceuta just steps from the moat.
Look for masonry sections dating back to the Portuguese period before Spanish rule, visible in parts of the moat walls.

activities.text: Cross into the old town via the fortified gate
activities.detail: Pass through the original gateway that separates the peninsula from the mainland, a dramatic transition point in the city's layout.

food.detail: Try Ceuta's classic fried squid, a popular tapa found in bars near the historic center after visiting the walls.
Sample an assortment of small fried fish typical of the Ceuta and Andalusian coastal tradition.
Enjoy sweet mint tea served with almond pastries, reflecting the city's blend of Spanish and Moroccan culinary influences.
Grab a squid sandwich from a nearby café, a casual local favorite for a quick bite near the fortifications.
Look for a local salad variant often mixing tuna, olives, and peppers, common in eateries around Plaza de África.

culture.detail: The moat and walls are protected heritage sites, so avoid climbing on restricted stone structures.
Lighting is limited at night, so early morning or late afternoon visits offer the best views and photo opportunities.
Remember that this moat historically marked the boundary between the Spanish enclave and Moroccan territory, giving it geopolitical significance.
Unlike some fortress sites, the exterior moat area is generally free to explore, though museum sections may charge a small fee.

places.name: Museum of the Legion
places.description: A military museum dedicated to the Spanish Legion, founded in Ceuta in the 1920s, displaying uniforms, weapons and historical artifacts. It offers insight into a distinctive chapter of Spanish military history tied closely to the city.

activities.detail: See personal memorabilia and photographs of Millán Astray and José Millán, the founders of the Spanish Legion in 1920.
Examine an extensive collection of rifles, bayonets and artillery pieces used by the Legion throughout the 20th century.
Study the evolution of Legion uniforms, including the iconic chapiri cap and the distinctive green and red sash.
See original regimental banners carried during campaigns in Morocco and the Spanish Civil War.
Learn about the Legion's famous goat mascot tradition, a symbol paraded during military ceremonies.
Inspect historic cannons and vehicles displayed in the museum's open-air courtyard.

food.detail: Try a reenactment-style hearty stew similar to the traditional field ration once served to Legion troops.
Sip sweet Moroccan-influenced mint tea, a popular refreshment in Ceuta reflecting its North African ties.
Grab a fried squid sandwich from a nearby Ceuta café, a favorite quick bite for visitors touring military sites.
Sample this local sweet pastry often enjoyed with coffee after a museum visit.

culture.detail: As an active barracks site, visitors should remain quiet and respectful, especially during flag ceremonies.
Tours are often led by Legion veterans who provide firsthand historical commentary rarely found in guidebooks.
Some areas prohibit photography due to the museum's location within an active military installation.
Given the site's solemn military character, casual beachwear or overly casual attire is discouraged.

places.name: Melilla
places.description: A Spanish enclave on Morocco's Mediterranean coast renowned for its exceptional collection of Modernist architecture and layered multicultural history. Its blend of colonial-era buildings, fortifications and vibrant street life make it a uniquely rewarding destination.

activities.detail: Explore this compact fortified old town with its labyrinth of ramparts, gates and hidden squares overlooking the Mediterranean.

activities.text: Admire Modernist buildings on Calle General Marina
activities.detail: Stroll past early 20th-century Art Nouveau facades designed largely by Enrique Nieto, a former collaborator of Gaudí.

activities.text: Visit the Museo Militar in Fuerte de la Purísima Concepción
activities.detail: Housed within the old fortress walls, this museum traces Melilla's long military and colonial history.

activities.text: Descend into the Cuevas del Conventico
activities.detail: Explore a network of tunnels carved into the cliffside once used for defense and storage during sieges.

activities.text: See the Palacio de la Asamblea
activities.detail: Photograph this striking Neo-Baroque city hall, one of the city's most recognizable Modernist landmarks.
Enjoy this shaded central park with palm trees, fountains and a lively café scene at its edges.
Visit one of Spain's oldest bullrings, a distinctive circular structure reflecting the city's Andalusian heritage.

activities.text: Cross to the Moroccan border at Beni Enzar
activities.detail: Take a short trip to the frontier to observe the striking daily flow of people and goods between Spain and Morocco.

food.detail: Skewered, spiced grilled meat reflecting Melilla's deep Moroccan culinary influence, sold at street stalls and bars alike.
A local take on North African couscous, often served with vegetables and slow-cooked lamb or chicken.
Crispy fried eggplant slices drizzled with honey, a beloved Andalusian-Sephardic-inspired tapa found across the city.
Sweet mint tea served in small glasses alongside almond pastries, a daily ritual blending Spanish and Moroccan customs.
Small fried or baked pastries filled with tuna, meat or vegetables, popular as a quick snack throughout the city.

culture.detail: Melilla's population includes Christian, Muslim, Jewish and Hindu communities, so respectful curiosity about diverse traditions is appreciated.
Despite its proximity to Morocco, Melilla uses the euro exclusively, unlike some informal cross-border transactions nearby.
If visiting Morocco via Beni Enzar, allow extra time for potentially long queues and document checks.
Many shops and some sites close in the early afternoon, reopening later in the evening, especially in summer.

places.name: Old Melilla
places.description: A fortified old town perched on a rocky promontory, comprising four walled enclosures built from the 16th century onward. Narrow lanes, chapels, museums and sea bastions make it the historic heart of the city.

activities.detail: Pass beneath this restored 16th-century Gothic gateway, one of the original entrances into the fortress town.

activities.text: Tour the Museo Municipal de Melilla
activities.detail: Housed inside the Baluarte de la Concepción bastion, this museum traces Phoenician, Roman, Islamic and Spanish layers of the city's history.

activities.text: Visit the Iglesia de la Purísima Concepción
activities.detail: This baroque sanctuary honors Melilla's patroness and sits at the heart of the old quarter's religious life.

activities.text: Step into the Capilla de Santiago
activities.detail: A rare Gothic chapel built into the walls, one of the few surviving from the original 15th-16th century fortification.
Follow the dry moat and bastion walkways for sweeping views over the Mediterranean and the modern city below.
This keep tower offers a sense of the fortress's defensive scale and panoramic coastal views.
Wander through tunnels and caves carved into the rock that once served as storage and refuge within the fortress.

activities.text: Cross through the four walled enclosures
activities.detail: Trace the layered recintos built progressively from the 16th century, each added as the town's defenses expanded seaward.

food.detail: A sweet-savory filo pie of pigeon or chicken, almonds and cinnamon, reflecting Melilla's Sephardic and Moroccan culinary blend.
Fried eggplant slices drizzled with honey, a Sephardic Jewish dish still served in cafes near the old town.
Almond-filled crescent pastries dusted with sugar, a classic Andalusi-Maghrebi sweet found in local bakeries.
Sweet mint tea served with almond pastries, best enjoyed at a terrace overlooking the fortress walls.
Fresh seafood caught just below the ramparts, served grilled or fried in nearby portside restaurants.

culture.detail: Melilla's old town reflects Christian, Jewish, Muslim and Hindu communities living side by side, so dress and behave respectfully near religious sites.
The steep cobbled lanes and stairways of the fortress can be uneven and slippery, especially after rain.
Many sites keep limited opening hours and close on Mondays, so plan visits in advance.

culture.text: Visit by day for full access
culture.detail: Some bastions and passages are gated at night, so daytime exploration offers the best access to the walled enclosures.

places.name: Plaza de España
places.description: The central square of modern Melilla, surrounded by grand early 20th-century buildings including the Palacio de la Asamblea. It serves as the social and architectural showcase of the city's Modernist heritage.

activities.text: Admire the Palacio de la Asamblea
activities.detail: Marvel at Melilla's Modernist city hall, designed by Enrique Nieto, a disciple of Gaudí, with its ornate facade and clock tower.

activities.text: Visit the Monument to the Fallen
activities.detail: See the tall obelisk war memorial standing at the center of the square, a key gathering point in the city.
Wander through the adjoining subtropical garden with ponds, sculptures, and shaded paths just steps from the plaza.
Head across the bridge over the old moat to explore the fortified old town rising just beyond the square.
Look up at the surrounding early 20th-century buildings that showcase Melilla's rich Art Nouveau architectural heritage.
Join locals for the traditional evening stroll around the square once the buildings are illuminated.

food.text: Café con leche on a terrace
food.detail: Sit at one of the plaza's outdoor cafés for a classic Spanish coffee while people-watching.
Try sweet Moroccan-style mint tea, a nod to Melilla's cross-cultural identity, served at nearby cafés.
Sample the local take on Russian salad, often served as a tapa in bars around the square.
Seek out this Moroccan-influenced savory-sweet pastry filled with meat, almonds, and cinnamon, found in local bakeries.
Hop between the bars lining the plaza for small plates of seafood, jamón, and local specialties.

culture.detail: The Palacio de la Asamblea and surrounding buildings are beautifully lit at night, making evening the best time to see the square.
The Monument to the Fallen is a solemn site, so keep noise and behavior respectful when nearby.
Many Modernist architecture tours of Melilla begin here, so it's a good spot to pick up a map or guide.
Notice the mix of Spanish and Amazigh-Moroccan influences in the cafés and shops surrounding the plaza.

places.name: Modernist Quarter & Gran Hotel
places.nameIt: Barrio Modernista y Gran Hotel Melilla
places.description: A district filled with Art Nouveau facades designed largely by disciples of Gaudí, anchored by the elegant early 20th-century Gran Hotel. It is one of Spain's richest concentrations of Modernist architecture outside Catalonia.

activities.detail: Admire this elegant early 20th-century hotel, a centerpiece of the city's Modernist heritage with ornate balconies and a grand facade.
Stroll through this central square surrounded by Modernist buildings and lush gardens that anchor the district's atmosphere.
Photograph the striking twin-towered city hall, one of Enrique Nieto's most iconic Modernist works.
Step inside this Neo-Gothic church with Modernist decorative touches, an important religious landmark in the quarter.
Wander this street lined with private Modernist villas showcasing wrought-iron balconies and floral stone carvings.
Admire the Art Deco and Modernist facade of this historic theater, still used for cultural events today.
Follow a self-guided or guided walk tracing the works of Gaudí's disciple Enrique Nieto scattered across the quarter.
View this ornate former military social club, a fine example of eclectic Modernist design in Melilla.

food.detail: Try these grilled skewers of spiced marinated meat, a local favorite reflecting Melilla's North African influence.
Sip sweet mint tea paired with almond pastries in a café near the Gran Hotel, a tradition blending Spanish and Maghrebi customs.
Enjoy freshly fried churros with thick hot chocolate at a historic café in the Modernist quarter.
Sample these crescent-shaped almond pastries dusted with sugar, a sweet influenced by the city's Sephardic and Muslim communities.
Taste this local variation of Spanish potato salad, often served as a tapa in bars around Plaza de España.

culture.detail: Melilla's population includes Christian, Muslim, Jewish, and Hindu communities, so dress modestly and be respectful near religious sites.
Visit the Modernist facades in late afternoon when the sun highlights the intricate stone and ironwork details.
Many shops and some cafés close in early afternoon, so plan sightseeing around midday closures.
Local tourism offices offer guided walks explaining Enrique Nieto's Modernist legacy, well worth booking in advance.

places.name: Cape Tres Forcas
places.description: A dramatic rocky cape near Melilla with cliffs, a historic lighthouse and turquoise waters. It's a scenic escape for hiking and coastal views away from the urban center.

activities.text: Visit the Cabo Tres Forcas lighthouse
activities.detail: This solitary 19th-century lighthouse marks the tip of the peninsula and offers sweeping views over the Alboran Sea.
A rugged dirt track along the ridge winds past volcanic rock formations and drops to hidden coves.
Small turquoise-watered coves below the cliffs are ideal for a quiet swim away from Melilla's busier beaches.

activities.text: Watch the sunset over the strait
activities.detail: The exposed western point gives unobstructed views of the sun dropping toward the Rif mountains and the Mediterranean.
The cape's isolation makes it a resting point for migratory seabirds crossing between Africa and Europe.
Dark basalt outcrops and eroded sea stacks create striking contrasts against the bright blue water.

food.detail: Freshly caught sardines cooked over open coals are a simple staple enjoyed by fishermen and visitors near the shore.
A sweet, strong mint tea often shared at small roadside stalls or with local fishermen taking a break.
Nearby coastal villages prepare tagines with the day's catch, simmered with tomatoes, olives, and preserved lemon.
Before or after visiting the cape, travelers often pick up Spanish-Moroccan fusion pastries in Melilla, blending both culinary traditions.

culture.text: Respect the military and fishing presence
culture.detail: Parts of the cape are used by local fishermen and occasionally patrolled, so visitors should stay on marked paths and be courteous.
There are no shops or restaurants directly at the cape, so pack water and food before heading out.
The road to the lighthouse is unpaved and best suited to a sturdy vehicle or a long hike, especially after rain.
Since the cape lies in Moroccan territory near the Spanish enclave of Melilla, travelers should carry proper identification when moving between zones.

places.name: Carabos Beach
places.description: A popular sandy beach in Melilla along the Mediterranean promenade, ideal for swimming and relaxing with views of the marina. It's a favorite local spot for sun and sea combined with easy city access.

activities.detail: The beach's gentle waves and shallow entry make it a safe, popular spot for swimming most of the year.
Walk the palm-lined promenade that runs alongside the beach, connecting it to Melilla's marina and city center.

activities.text: Watch the boats at Melilla Marina
activities.detail: Admire yachts and fishing boats docked at the nearby marina just steps from the sand.
Settle in with a beach chair and umbrella rented from seasonal kiosks right on the sand.
Grab an outdoor table at one of the small beach bars for a front-row seat to local beach life.
Evenings bring soft light over the Mediterranean, a favorite time for locals to gather along the shore.

food.detail: A classic seaside snack sold at nearby chiringuitos, simply grilled and served with lemon.
Small plates of local shrimp, calamari, and fish reflecting Melilla's Spanish-Mediterranean-North African fusion.
Melilla's mixed heritage brings honey-soaked pastries you can find in cafés just off the beach promenade.
A refreshing iced drink popular on hot beach days, sold from kiosks along the seafront.
Lightly battered mixed fried fish, a staple of Spanish coastal dining enjoyed at beachfront restaurants.

culture.detail: Locals flock here on weekends and holidays, so arrive early to claim a good spot on the sand.
Melilla blends Spanish and North African customs, so modest swimwear etiquette is appreciated away from the immediate shoreline.
The adjacent paseo is ideal for an evening walk after the beach, with cafés and shops nearby.
Bring sunscreen and a hat, as the Mediterranean sun can be strong even in shoulder seasons.

restaurants.name: Restaurante El Ancla
restaurants.description: Iconic seafood restaurant overlooking the port with fresh catches prepared in traditional Spanish style.
restaurants.mustOrder: Espetos de sardinas a la sal

restaurants.name: Casa Pepe
restaurants.description: Casual family-run spot serving authentic Ceutí cuisine with views of the main square and excellent value.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Príncipe
restaurants.description: Well-established restaurant known for hearty Spanish dishes and a welcoming atmosphere popular with locals.

restaurants.name: La Tranca
restaurants.description: Lively tapas bar perfect for casual dining with excellent jamón ibérico and local wines at budget-friendly prices.
restaurants.mustOrder: Tabla de jamón y quesos ibéricos

restaurants.name: Restaurante Bab Taza
restaurants.description: Charming fusion restaurant blending Moroccan and Spanish influences reflecting Ceuta's unique cultural position.

restaurants.name: El Pescador
restaurants.description: Long-established fish restaurant famous for its daily catch and traditional preparation methods.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Mar y Tierra
restaurants.description: Modern restaurant with panoramic port views serving creative interpretations of Mediterranean classics.

restaurants.name: Restaurante La Alcazaba
restaurants.description: Elegant restaurant in Melilla's old town featuring quality regional ingredients and refined presentation.

restaurants.name: Casa Luján
restaurants.description: Popular family restaurant serving generous portions of classic Spanish fare in a relaxed, unpretentious setting.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Mikasa
restaurants.description: Unexpectedly excellent Japanese restaurant offering sushi, sashimi, and hot dishes prepared by skilled chefs.

restaurants.name: Bar Restaurante El Faro
restaurants.description: Waterfront bar-restaurant perfect for casual seafood dining with local catch and relaxed maritime atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Zoco
restaurants.description: Authentic Moroccan restaurant celebrating Melilla's proximity to Morocco with traditional tagines and couscous.
restaurants.mustOrder: Tajín de pollo con limón y aceitunas

restaurants.name: Restaurante El Peñón
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant with creative presentations of local ingredients and an extensive wine selection.

restaurants.name: Tasca La Esquinita
restaurants.description: Charming corner tavern ideal for standing at the bar with a drink and sampling traditional small plates.
